title: On the Connection Between Differential Population Growth Rate and Epidemic Reproduction Numbers
summary: During pandemics, public health agencies need to rapidly assess whether a new viral variant is more transmissible than existing lineages. For co-circulating variants, relative fitness can be expressed as a selective coefficient, as the differential population growth rate (DPGR) estimated from genomic surveillance, or, with additional assumptions, as a contrast in epidemic reproduction numbers $R_t$. We show that DPGR estimates a pairwise growth-rate difference. Under a specified generation-interval model, this difference can be transformed into reproduction-number space; in the equal-generation-time SIR special case, it reduces to a scaled difference in variant-specific $R_t$. Related growth-rate contrasts also appear in multinomial logistic and growth-advantage random-walk models, although those methods differ from DPGR in likelihood, smoothing, priors, and data inputs. We evaluate the theory across five SARS-CoV-2 and influenza analyses totaling more than 2,200 matched data points. SIR simulation recovers the expected mapping when the true $R_t$ is known, and retrospective SARS-CoV-2 analyses show sustained DPGR signals 43 to 65 days before variant dominance, with 95\% sign accuracy in our analysis. DPGR is approximately transitive across lineage triplets, near zero for selected functionally similar sublineages, and directionally consistent across countries. These results connect sequence-count-based fitness estimates to reproduction-number contrasts through an assumption-explicit growth-rate bridge.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that Differential Population Growth Rate (DPGR) estimates pairwise growth-rate differences between viral variants, can be linked to reproduction number contrasts via a generation-interval model, and demonstrates robust early signals of variant fitness advantage across SARS-CoV-2 and influenza data.
AI.motivation: To enable rapid assessment of whether a new viral variant is more transmissible than existing lineages during pandemics, using genomic surveillance data.
AI.method: DPGR estimates pairwise growth-rate differences, transformable to reproduction-number space under a generation-interval model; validated across five viral analyses with over 2,200 data points, including SIR simulations and retrospective SARS-CoV-2 studies.
AI.result: DPGR signals appear 43-65 days before variant dominance with 95% sign accuracy; approximately transitive across lineage triplets, near zero for functionally similar sublineages, and directionally consistent across countries.
AI.conclusion: Sequence-count-based fitness estimates can be connected to reproduction-number contrasts through an assumption-explicit growth-rate bridge.

title: A Novel Computer Vision Approach for Assessing Fish Responses to Intrusive Objects in Aquaculture
summary:
The aquaculture industry needs to address several challenges to secure sustainable seafood production that can serve an increasing global demand. One major challenge is to ensure good fish health and acceptable welfare during production since the improvement of fish welfare is of vital importance in current and future production systems. In this study, this is addressed by developing and implementing methods to identify fish behaviors in response to intrusive objects both on individual and on a group basis. A novel approach for detecting, tracking, and estimating the 3D position of individual fish has thus been developed, and specifically designed to track the caudal fins of farmed fish in industrial sea cages. The tracking data was subjected to a novel stereo-vision method adapted to estimate fish positions, velocities, accelerations, and turning and pitch angles. Datasets obtained from industrial-scale fish farms were then analyzed to identify the impact of structures of varying shapes, sizes, and colors on fish behavior.
  The method was trained using manually labeled caudal fins, and used YOLOv8 with ByteTrack as an object detector and tracker, SuperGlue for matching detections in the left and right frames, and triangulation to reconstruct the 3D positions of the fish. Different image pre-processing and augmentation methods for enhancing object detection accuracy were tested and their performance compared, while RAFT-Stereo was tested for depth estimation purposes. The obtained results both validate the method's performance against previous research efforts, and demonstrate the novelty and potential of this method in providing more insight into behavioral dynamics in sea-cages.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a stereo-vision method using YOLOv8, ByteTrack, SuperGlue, and triangulation to track individual fish caudal fins in 3D within industrial sea cages, enabling analysis of fish behavior in response to intrusive objects for welfare monitoring.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in aquaculture for sustainable seafood production, specifically improving fish welfare by understanding fish behavioral responses to intrusive objects in sea cages.
AI.method: A stereo-vision approach combining YOLOv8 with ByteTrack for detection/tracking, SuperGlue for stereo matching, and triangulation for 3D reconstruction. Tested various image pre-processing/augmentation methods and RAFT-Stereo for depth estimation. Trained on manually labeled caudal fins.
AI.result: The method was validated against previous research, demonstrating effectiveness in estimating 3D positions, velocities, accelerations, turning and pitch angles of fish in industrial-scale sea cages.
AI.conclusion: The novel method provides valuable insights into behavioral dynamics in sea-cages, contributing to improved fish welfare monitoring in aquaculture production systems.

title: Meta-analysis of scRNA-seq data for choroidal endothelial cells in dry Age-related Macular Degeneration
summary: The mechanisms that lead to dry Age-related Macular Degeneration are largely unelucidated, which prevents the introduction of effective therapies. Experimental support exists in the literature for the hypothesis that choroidal endothelial cell (ChEC) dysfunction precedes the loss of macular retinal pigmented epithelial (RPE), which may be only a secondary consequence of inadequate blood supply. If so, interventions at the level of ChEC could constitute an under investigated therapeutic strategy. Datasets regarding the transcriptional changes in early or intermediate dry AMD are publicly available, but for some some of them the information about ChECs have not been analyzed, or not analyzed using the most powerful and recent software tools. We present here new data generated by our bioinformatics analysis of these datasets. The main new finding is that angiogenesis is initiated in dry AMD, as it is in wet AMD. However, contrary to wet AMD, in dry AMD angiogenesis fails to execute, and therefore the blood supply that supports the RPE becomes gradually insufficient, leading to their dysfunctionality and death. The data support a unitary hypothesis of the origin / initiation / etiology of both dry and wet AMD, namely that both are initiated by ChEC dysfunction - either insufficient / abortive angiogenesis in dry AMD, or excessive angiogenesis in wet AMD. Pathway analysis also reveals as perturbed Notch and TNF signaling, endothelial to mesenchymal transition (EndoMT), mitochondria, "fluid shear stress", "osteoclast differentiation" and "calcification/osteoporosis". Overall, the new data provide a rationale for experimental studies, to validate and further characterize these perturbations, and investigate strategies to correct them.

AI.tldr: Bioinformatics analysis of public datasets reveals that dry AMD, like wet AMD, initiates with angiogenesis, but in dry AMD this angiogenesis fails to execute, leading to insufficient blood supply and RPE death. This supports a unified hypothesis that both forms of AMD originate from choroidal endothelial cell dysfunction.
AI.motivation: The mechanisms of dry AMD are poorly understood, preventing effective therapies. The authors aim to investigate the hypothesis that ChEC dysfunction precedes RPE loss, and that ChEC-targeted interventions may be a viable but underexplored therapeutic strategy.
AI.method: Bioinformatics re-analysis of publicly available transcriptomic datasets from early/intermediate dry AMD patients, using modern software tools to extract previously overlooked information about choroidal endothelial cells.
AI.result: Angiogenesis is initiated in dry AMD (like wet AMD) but fails to execute, causing insufficient blood supply. Pathway analysis reveals perturbations in Notch, TNF, EndoMT, mitochondria, shear stress, osteoclast differentiation, and calcification pathways.
AI.conclusion: Both dry and wet AMD share a common origin: ChEC dysfunction. In dry AMD, this manifests as abortive angiogenesis; in wet AMD, as excessive angiogenesis. The findings provide rationale for future experimental validation and potential ChEC-directed therapies.

title: Gaussian Mixture Model-Based Focused Refinement for Enhanced Flexible Structure Determination in CryoEM and CryoET
summary: Dynamic conformational changes of proteins are crucial for their cellular functions. Here we present a unified refinement pipeline for flexible protein structures in both CryoEM and in situ CryoET. Using a Gaussian mixture model-based focused alignment procedure, we improve resolution of small domains in highly dynamic proteins and reveal intricate conformational changes. The method corrects the per-subunit motion of TRPV1 and captures the rotary dynamics of ATP synthase within mitochondria.

AI.tldr: A unified refinement pipeline using Gaussian mixture model-based focused alignment improves resolution of dynamic protein domains in CryoEM and CryoET, demonstrated on TRPV1 and ATP synthase.
AI.motivation: Dynamic conformational changes of proteins are crucial for cellular functions but difficult to resolve with existing methods.
AI.method: A Gaussian mixture model-based focused alignment procedure within a unified refinement pipeline for flexible protein structures in CryoEM and in situ CryoET.
AI.result: Improved resolution of small domains in highly dynamic proteins, revealing intricate conformational changes; corrected per-subunit motion of TRPV1 and captured rotary dynamics of ATP synthase within mitochondria.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline enables better visualization of dynamic conformational changes in flexible proteins, particularly for small domains, using both CryoEM and in situ CryoET.

title: High-Fidelity 3D Simulator for Synthetic fNIRS Data Generation
summary: Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) provides a noninvasive window into brain activity by measuring task-related changes in oxygenated and deoxygenated hemoglobin in the cortex. A key advantage of fNIRS is its promise of use with mobile participants in complex, real-world environments, such as walking, sports, classroom learning, driving simulations, or social interactions. However, analyzing fNIRS data is challenging because of motion artifacts, physiological noise, and other confounding factors. This challenge is further compounded by the limited availability of annotated datasets, which hinders the development and validation of new analysis pipelines, particularly given the growing use of AI methods. Recognizing these challenges, we introduce a 3D fNIRS simulator that uses mesh-based Monte Carlo simulations to create physiologically realistic, full-head synthetic recordings with high spatiotemporal fidelity. Our simulator combines anatomically accurate sensitivity profiles with parameterized models of hemodynamic responses, systemic physiology, and nonsystematic artifacts. As a result, users can generate virtually unlimited labeled datasets for testing denoising algorithms, data augmentation, mechanistic modeling, or \textit{in silico} experimentation. We validate the simulator using experimental fNIRS data from open-source finger-tapping, pain-assessment, and surgical-skill datasets and provide an open-source implementation to support reproducibility and broad adoption.

AI.tldr: A 3D fNIRS simulator using mesh-based Monte Carlo methods generates realistic synthetic brain recordings with ground truth labels, validated on three experimental datasets, to support algorithm development and in silico experimentation.
AI.motivation: fNIRS data analysis is hindered by motion artifacts, physiological noise, and especially the scarcity of annotated datasets, which limits the development and validation of AI-based analysis pipelines.
AI.method: A 3D simulator combining mesh-based Monte Carlo simulations with anatomically accurate sensitivity profiles and parameterized models of hemodynamic responses, systemic physiology, and artifacts to generate labeled synthetic full-head fNIRS recordings.
AI.result: Validated against experimental fNIRS data from finger-tapping, pain-assessment, and surgical-skill datasets; open-source implementation provided.
AI.conclusion: The simulator enables virtually unlimited labeled data generation for denoising, data augmentation, mechanistic modeling, and reproducible in silico fNIRS research.

title: Obesity and Sociodemographic Factors in Luminal Breast Cancer
summary: Luminal breast cancers represent the most prevalent molecular subtype of breast carcinoma, with Luminal A tumors generally associated with more favorable clinical outcomes than Luminal B tumors. Obesity-related inflammation and prolonged exposure to exogenous steroids have been implicated in the progression of luminal malignancies. This study evaluated 1,928 patients with Luminal A breast cancer and 1,610 patients with Luminal B breast cancer to examine associations among body mass index (BMI), age, ethnic background, menopausal status, and receptor expression, including estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). Patients with Luminal B tumors demonstrated a significantly greater mean BMI compared with those with Luminal A tumors. In addition, Luminal B tumors were more frequently observed among patients of African ancestry relative to White and Hispanic populations. Multivariable analyses revealed that elevated BMI and African ancestry were independently associated with increased odds of Luminal B carcinoma, whereas postmenopausal status was associated with lower risk. Mediation analysis further indicated that BMI partially explained the association between ancestry and Luminal B disease. These findings suggest that obesity and population-specific factors may contribute to the development of more aggressive luminal breast cancer phenotypes.

AI.tldr: This study of 3,538 patients found that higher BMI and African ancestry are independently associated with Luminal B (more aggressive) breast cancer, while postmenopausal status is protective. BMI partially mediates the ancestry-Luminal B relationship.
AI.motivation: To investigate how obesity, demographic factors, and receptor expression relate to the more aggressive Luminal B vs. Luminal A breast cancer subtypes.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 1,928 Luminal A and 1,610 Luminal B patients; assessed associations among BMI, age, ethnicity, menopausal status, and receptor expression using multivariable and mediation analyses.
AI.result: Luminal B patients had higher BMI; Luminal B was more frequent in African ancestry patients; elevated BMI and African ancestry independently increased odds of Luminal B; postmenopausal status decreased risk; BMI partially mediated ancestry-Luminal B link.
AI.conclusion: Obesity and population-specific factors contribute to more aggressive luminal breast cancer phenotypes.

title: The Geometry of Activity Cliffs: Representation Dependence and Multi-Scale Characterization of Activity Landscapes
summary:
Activity cliffs, structurally similar compounds with large potency differences, are widely treated as intrinsic features of chemical datasets. We argue that apart from target biology, much of our cliff understanding is a consequence of the geometry induced by the chosen molecular representation, not a property of a molecule pair itself.
  We designed a six-step pipeline to systematically test this hypothesis. The pipeline consists of: assessing pairwise distance geometry, cliff enrichment, activity gradient distribution, persistent homology of the cliff subspace, predictive benchmarking for a chosen pair of an embedding and a metric, and eventually, analysis of the matched molecular pairs and stereoisomers. We applied the pipeline to fifteen configurations of embeddings and metrics to build a benchmark across three distinctive datasets known of activity cliffs challenges.
  No representation excels on all criteria: Morgan Tanimoto provides the strongest cliff enrichment and cross-scaffold generalization; MolFormer cosine provides the only meaningful stereochemical sensitivity; MACCS and RDKit Dice fingerprints are most sensitive to matched-molecular-pair transformations; ChemBERTa fails uniformly due to embedding collapse.
  These findings are not a ranking. They reflect the fact that different representations encode different aspects of molecular recognition, and that choosing one implicitly defines what an activity cliff actually is.

AI.tldr: Activity cliffs are not just biological properties but are largely defined by the choice of molecular representation; no single embedding excels across all criteria, and different representations encode different aspects of molecular recognition.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that activity cliffs are purely intrinsic features of chemical datasets, and to demonstrate that they are significantly shaped by the geometric properties of the chosen molecular representation.
AI.method: Designed a six-step pipeline evaluating 15 embedding-metric configurations across three datasets, assessing pairwise distance geometry, cliff enrichment, activity gradient distribution, persistent homology, predictive benchmarking, and matched molecular pair/stereoisomer analysis.
AI.result: Morgan Tanimoto: best cliff enrichment and cross-scaffold generalization; MolFormer cosine: only meaningful stereochemical sensitivity; MACCS and RDKit Dice: most sensitive to matched-molecular-pair transformations; ChemBERTa: failed uniformly due to embedding collapse.
AI.conclusion: No representation universally superior; the definition and detection of activity cliffs are implicit consequences of the representation chosen, not objective properties of molecule pairs themselves.

title: Extended predictive coding framework as variational free-energy minimisation under exponential-family assumption
summary: The sensory cortices of the brain perform perceptual inference efficiently through their complex networks of neurons. One of the theoretical accounts of this process is the free-energy principle (FEP), which postulates that the brain performs variational Bayesian inference. Pioneering studies have shown that FEP can correspond to the predictive coding (PC) hypothesis under the Gaussian assumption and Laplace approximation. However, PC-based implementations of FEP within such a limited Gaussian regime have failed to capture several properties of biological neural networks, such as nonlinearity and heterogeneity of input--output properties within a network, and the biological implausibility of negative firing rates. This study shows that, when a broader class of probability distributions, namely the exponential family of distributions (EFD), is assumed for the variational posterior and prior, these missing characteristics are exhibited within the network, maintaining the FEP--PC correspondence up to the second cumulant of the posterior. We also show that the proposed model can be trained by biologically plausible local plasticity rules. Our results enrich the explanatory power of FEP regarding neural dynamics involved in perception as variational inference.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the free-energy principle to exponential family distributions, showing that nonlinearity, heterogeneity, and non-negative firing rates emerge naturally in neural networks while maintaining correspondence with predictive coding.
AI.motivation: Existing PC-based implementations of FEP rely on Gaussian assumptions and Laplace approximations, which fail to capture nonlinearity, heterogeneity of input-output properties, and biological implausibility of negative firing rates in neural networks.
AI.method: The authors assume exponential family distributions (EFD) for variational posterior and prior, maintaining FEP-PC correspondence up to the second cumulant, and demonstrate training via biologically plausible local plasticity rules.
AI.result: The proposed model exhibits nonlinearity, heterogeneity, and avoids negative firing rates, all missing in Gaussian-based FEP implementations.
AI.conclusion: The work enriches the explanatory power of FEP for neural dynamics in perception as variational inference, bridging theory and biological realism.

title: Coordination without communication: beyond optimisation and geometric Brownian motion
summary: We introduce a physically grounded framework for coordination in a population based on information constrained feedback in a partially observed stochastic dynamical system. Population size evolves as a continuous time birth death Markov process whose transition rates respond to a shared stochastic measurement signal correlated with the underlying population state. Individuals neither communicate directly nor optimise strategies; instead, coordination emerges from macro to micro feedback mediated by imperfect common information. We show that geometric Brownian motion arises as a limiting case of the conditional dynamics when measurement strength and population statistics satisfy suitable conditions. More generally, varying the signal to noise properties of the measurement channel produces a wider class of stochastic growth processes, including diffusive and jump like regimes, even though ensemble average growth remains exponential. In an appropriate limit the framework recovers the stochastic multiplicative growth model of Peters and Adamou, providing a physical interpretation of coordination as inference and feedback under partial observability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a physically grounded framework where coordination in a population emerges from information-constrained feedback in a partially observed stochastic system, without direct communication or optimization. It shows how geometric Brownian motion and broader stochastic growth processes arise as limiting cases, with coordination interpreted as inference and feedback under partial observability.
AI.motivation: To develop a physically grounded understanding of how coordination can emerge spontaneously in a population without direct communication or strategic optimization, through macro-to-micro feedback mediated by imperfect common information.
AI.method: The authors model a continuous-time birth-death Markov process where transition rates respond to a shared stochastic measurement signal correlated with the underlying population state. They analyze the conditional dynamics under varying signal-to-noise properties of the measurement channel.
AI.result: Geometric Brownian motion emerges as a limiting case. Varying signal-to-noise properties produces diffusive, jump-like, and other stochastic growth regimes while ensemble-average growth remains exponential. The framework recovers the stochastic multiplicative growth model of Peters and Adamou in an appropriate limit.
AI.conclusion: Coordination can be physically interpreted as inference and feedback under partial observability, providing a unified framework connecting information theory, stochastic processes, and emergent collective behavior.

title: AMix-2: Establishing Protein as a Native Modality in Large Language Models
summary: We present AMix-2, a protein-text foundation model that establishes protein as a native modality in large language models (LLMs), unifying protein understanding and sequence design within a single foundation model. AMix-2 is built upon two key ideas: (1) a unified protein-text formulation that embeds natural language and protein sequence in a shared token space, enabling one model to perform biological reasoning and conditional design instead of separate downstream task-specialized models; and (2) a block-wise diffusion language modeling backbone that combines causal generation across blocks with bidirectional context and iterative refinement within blocks. This scheme better matches the intrinsic nature of proteins than a strict left-to-right factorization. To evaluate protein foundation models under realistic generalization settings, we further introduce ProteinArena, a comprehensive benchmark with time-aware and homology-aware protocols across various understanding and design tasks, and with baselines covering classical bioinformatics tools, protein-specialized models and LLMs. On ProteinArena, AMix-2 outperforms frontier LLMs and demonstrates competitive performance to task-specific protein models. Controlled experiments further show that the diffusion-based paradigm generally surpasses its autoregressive counterpart, highlighting the advantage of flexible generation order for protein sequences. We release both AMix-2 and ProteinArena to facilitate open research in protein foundation models.

AI.tldr: AMix-2 is a protein-text foundation model that unifies protein understanding and sequence design using a shared token space and block-wise diffusion language modeling, outperforming frontier LLMs on the newly introduced ProteinArena benchmark.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of separate downstream task-specialized models and strict left-to-right autoregressive generation for protein sequences, by establishing protein as a native modality in LLMs with a more biologically natural generation order.
AI.method: (1) Unified protein-text formulation embedding natural language and protein sequences in a shared token space; (2) Block-wise diffusion language modeling backbone combining causal generation across blocks with bidirectional context and iterative refinement within blocks.
AI.result: Outperforms frontier LLMs and shows competitive performance to task-specific protein models on ProteinArena; diffusion-based paradigm generally surpasses autoregressive counterparts.
AI.conclusion: AMix-2 demonstrates that a single foundation model can unify protein understanding and design, with flexible generation order being advantageous for protein sequences. Both the model and benchmark are released for open research.

title: Morphological routes to extinction: A mechanistic assessment of habitat loss
summary: Habitat loss driven by climate and anthropogenic pressures alters patch morphology, with critical consequences for population persistence. Geometric and mechanistic metrics are commonly used to quantify degradation, yet their respective limitations remain poorly understood. Here, we address this gap using a reaction-diffusion framework for population growth and dispersal in a viable patch embedded in a hostile environment. We compare geometric descriptors of patch shape with a mechanistic metric derived from population growth near the extinction threshold. Along degradation trajectories, we find that geometric metrics systematically overestimate persistence, suggesting moderate and decelerating impacts, whereas mechanistic indicators reveal rapid, accelerating approaches to extinction. These results highlight fundamental limitations of geometric approaches and underscore the need for mechanistic assessments when evaluating biodiversity loss in complex landscapes.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that geometric metrics for measuring habitat degradation systematically overestimate population persistence compared to mechanistic metrics, which reveal more rapid and accelerating extinction risks.
AI.motivation: Habitat loss alters patch morphology affecting population persistence, but limitations of geometric vs. mechanistic metrics for quantifying degradation are poorly understood.
AI.method: A reaction-diffusion framework for population growth and dispersal in a viable patch within a hostile environment, comparing geometric shape descriptors with a mechanistic metric derived from population growth near extinction threshold.
AI.result: Geometric metrics overestimate persistence, showing moderate and decelerating impacts, while mechanistic indicators reveal rapid, accelerating approaches to extinction.
AI.conclusion: Geometric approaches have fundamental limitations; mechanistic assessments are needed when evaluating biodiversity loss in complex landscapes.

title: Analysis of a two patch model for disease vector-animal dynamics with non-linear anthropization-driven migration
summary: Landscape dynamics are key drivers of the movement and distribution of sylvatic hematophagous disease vectors and their (wild) animal hosts. Their habitats are undergoing increasing change, particularly fragmentation, through anthropogenic activity. In this article, we present and analyse a novel mathematical model that explicitly combines anthropization-induced landscape dynamics with the population dynamics of hematophagous vectors and (wild) animals dynamics. We develop a phenomenological and analytically tractable two-patch model in which the migration terms between the patches nonlinearly depend on the anthropization level of the patches. Our model analysis comprising analytical stability analysis and numerical bifurcation analysis provides information on how changes in model parameters, especially anthropization levels, shape the long-term dynamics in the model. Precisely, we find that low anthropogenic activity allows for a vector-animal coexistence state, while high anthropization leads to a vector extinction state. However, we establish that for intermediate anthropization levels, the transition between the two states is not necessarily monotonic, but may instead occur via a sequence of concurrent bifurcations along the anthropization axis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a mathematical two-patch model showing that low anthropization allows vector-animal coexistence, high anthropization causes vector extinction, and intermediate levels produce complex non-monotonic transitions via bifurcations.
AI.motivation: To understand how anthropogenic landscape fragmentation impacts the population dynamics of hematophagous disease vectors and their wild animal hosts.
AI.method: A phenomenological two-patch mathematical model with nonlinear migration terms depending on anthropization levels, analyzed via analytical stability and numerical bifurcation analysis.
AI.result: Low anthropization → vector-animal coexistence; high anthropization → vector extinction; intermediate anthropization → non-monotonic transitions via sequences of bifurcations.
AI.conclusion: Anthropization levels critically shape vector-host dynamics, and intermediate levels yield complex, non-trivial ecological transitions.

title: Quantifying biofilm-virulence index to predict antifungal resistance in Candida albicans
summary: Candida albicans is a commensal microorganism that causes opportunistic infections, such as oral candidiasis, vaginitis affecting females, newborns, and immunocompromised patients. Biofilm formation can lead to a commensal organism becoming a life-threatening organism by introducing antifungal resistance. The experiment we did combines crystal violet staining for biofilm biomass and CFU counts to statistically construct an additive BVI model by analysing the experimental data. Our study on the data proposes a Biofilm-Virulence Index (BVI) as a novel and quantitative parameter for assessing antifungal drug resistance in Candida albicans. The effect of the drugs on inhibition zone diameter is twofold, first, linear increase with time during early biofilm formation, second, stabilizing in later phases and correlating directly with virulence. Most BVI values remained in the mild infection range, indicating successful virulence reduction by antifungal drugs. The BVI model model combines the study of biofilm and viable cell count in a single parameter. So, this makes comparison between samples easier during biofilm analysis. Findings suggest that combination of CFU and biofilm measurement may improve interpretation of antifungal response in Candida albicans. This approach could be useful in future experimental studies investigating biofilm associated resistance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Biofilm-Virulence Index (BVI) that combines crystal violet staining (biofilm biomass) and CFU counts to quantitatively assess antifungal drug resistance in Candida albicans, showing that BVI values indicate successful virulence reduction and offering a single-parameter approach for easier comparison during biofilm analysis.
AI.motivation: Candida albicans biofilm formation introduces antifungal resistance, turning commensal organisms into life-threatening pathogens. Existing methods separately assess biofilm biomass and viable cell counts, lacking a unified quantitative parameter to evaluate antifungal drug resistance.
AI.method: Crystal violet staining (biofilm biomass) and CFU counts were combined. Experimental data were statistically analyzed to construct an additive BVI model. Inhibition zone diameters were measured over time during early and late biofilm formation phases.
AI.result: Drug inhibition zone diameter shows a twofold pattern: linear increase during early biofilm formation, then stabilization in later phases, correlating directly with virulence. Most BVI values fell within the mild infection range, indicating successful virulence reduction by antifungal drugs.
AI.conclusion: The BVI model effectively combines biofilm and viable cell count into a single parameter, simplifying sample comparison. Combining CFU and biofilm measurements improves interpretation of antifungal response, useful for future studies on biofilm-associated resistance.

title: SwitchCraft: A Programmatic Framework for Designing State-Switching Proteins
summary: Multistate mechanisms underlie many of the complex functions observed in natural proteins. The ability to rationally design multistate proteins would have transformative implications for many areas of biotechnology, yet lies beyond the capabilities of existing deep learning frameworks for protein design. To address this gap, we introduce SwitchCraft, a versatile and programmatic framework for designing state-switching proteins based on backpropagation through compositional design constraints parameterized by structure prediction models. In silico evaluations demonstrate success on a wide range of state-switching functional primitives, from allosteric regulation of motifs to discrimination of bound ligand identities. Using these primitives, we demonstrate an in silico strategy for de novo design of fluorescent biosensors to arbitrary small molecule analytes. These results position SwitchCraft at the inception of a powerful paradigm for higher-order functional protein design. Code is available at https://github.com/bjing2016/switchcraft.

AI.tldr: SwitchCraft is a programmatic framework for designing multistate (state-switching) proteins using backpropagation through structure prediction models, enabling allosteric regulation, ligand discrimination, and de novo fluorescent biosensor design.
AI.motivation: Natural proteins exhibit complex multistate mechanisms (e.g., allostery, conformational switching), but existing deep learning protein design frameworks cannot rationally design such multistate proteins.
AI.method: SwitchCraft uses backpropagation through compositional design constraints parameterized by structure prediction models to programmatically design state-switching proteins.
AI.result: Demonstrated in silico success on allosteric regulation of motifs, discrimination of bound ligand identities, and de novo design of fluorescent biosensors for arbitrary small molecules.
AI.conclusion: SwitchCraft establishes a new paradigm for higher-order functional protein design, enabling complex multistate behaviors beyond current deep learning capabilities.

title: mRNAutilus: Multi-Objective-Guided Discrete Generation of mRNA with Optimized Therapeutic Properties
summary: Therapeutic mRNA design requires coordinating multiple interacting sequence features across the full transcript, where codon usage, untranslated regions (UTRs), and their coupling jointly determine stability, translation efficiency, and protein expression. Here, we present mRNA generation via unrolled trajectories and informed latent updates (mRNAutilus), a framework for simultaneous codon optimization and de novo UTR design directly from sequence. mRNAutilus combines a masked discrete diffusion model trained on millions of full-length mRNAs with Monte Carlo Tree Guidance to generate Pareto-efficient sequences under multiple functional objectives, using lightweight regressors over model embeddings to predict half-life, translation efficiency, and protein abundance. Unlike recent methods that design coding sequences and UTRs separately or rely on post hoc assembly and screening, mRNAutilus generates complete transcripts in a single process optimized across properties. Across diverse targets, zero-shot mRNAs encoding P. pyralis luciferase achieve over 400-fold higher expression than wild-type and outperform commercial and machine learning-designed baselines, including zero-shot generative approaches. Zero-shot SARS-CoV-2 Spike mRNAs exceed clinically used and commercial constructs and match or surpass lab-optimized designs with improved durability. We further demonstrate generality in therapeutic settings, including prime editing (PEMax) and programmable proteome modulation, where mRNAutilus-designed constructs enhance expression of peptide-guided E3 ligases (uAbs) for beta-catenin degradation. These results establish a sequence-based, multi-objective framework for generating functional mRNAs tailored to diverse biological applications.

AI.tldr: mRNAutilus is a unified framework for simultaneous codon optimization and de novo UTR design using masked discrete diffusion with Monte Carlo Tree Guidance, generating complete mRNA transcripts with Pareto-efficient improvements across half-life, translation efficiency, and protein expression, achieving over 400-fold higher expression than wild-type in luciferase and outperforming clinical and lab-optimized SARS-CoV-2 Spike mRNA designs.
AI.motivation: Current methods design coding sequences and UTRs separately or rely on post hoc assembly and screening, failing to coordinate the interacting sequence features across the full transcript that jointly determine mRNA stability, translation efficiency, and protein expression.
AI.method: Combines a masked discrete diffusion model trained on millions of full-length mRNAs with Monte Carlo Tree Guidance to generate Pareto-efficient sequences under multiple objectives, using lightweight regressors over model embeddings to predict half-life, translation efficiency, and protein abundance.
AI.result: Zero-shot P. pyralis luciferase mRNAs achieve over 400-fold higher expression than wild-type, outperform commercial/ML baselines; zero-shot SARS-CoV-2 Spike mRNAs exceed clinically used constructs; demonstrates generality in prime editing (PEMax) and proteome modulation (uAbs for beta-catenin degradation).
AI.conclusion: A sequence-based, multi-objective framework that generates functional mRNAs tailored to diverse biological applications, establishing that simultaneous full-transcript optimization outperforms separate design and assembly approaches.

title: Consensus-level substitution rates are distinct from the virion-level rate
summary: Estimating viral substitution rates is central to evolutionary epidemiology, and recent interest in within-host evolution has sharpened the question of what such rates measure. I distinguish two classes of evolutionary rate estimand that are rarely separated in phylogenetic analysis: the virion-level substitution rate (VLSR), a molecular quantity counting mutational events along lineages, and consensus-level substitution rates (CLSRs), population-summary quantities counting changes in the consensus sequences. CLSRs are indexed by the consensus-generation rule. The VLSR and CLSRs are both biologically meaningful, but not interchangeable. Because the consensus-generation rule defines a given CLSR, it should be a routine reporting requirement. This reflection should help analysts make more informed methodological choices when working with sets of virus sequences.

AI.tldr: The paper distinguishes between two types of evolutionary rate estimates in viral phylogenetics—virion-level substitution rates (VLSR) and consensus-level substitution rates (CLSR)—and argues they are not interchangeable, urging routine reporting of the consensus-generation rule.
AI.motivation: To clarify a conceptual confusion in viral evolutionary epidemiology: the conflation of distinct evolutionary rate estimands (VLSR vs. CLSR) when analyzing within-host evolution.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis distinguishing two classes of substitution rate estimands: VLSR (molecular, lineage-level) and CLSR (population-level, consensus-dependent), with emphasis on the consensus-generation rule as a defining parameter.
AI.result: The VLSR and CLSRs are both biologically meaningful but not interchangeable; CLSRs are indexed by the consensus-generation rule, which should be routinely reported.
AI.conclusion: Analysts should make more informed methodological choices by distinguishing these rate types and reporting the consensus-generation rule as standard practice.

title: The Metastable Mind: Neural Underpinnings of Naturalistic Cognition Through the Synthesis of Event Segmentation and Metastable Neural States
summary: A multitude of findings and theories from cognitive, behavioural and computational neuroscience show that neural activity unfolds in a variety of meaningful temporal units. Behavioural research on event segmentation (ES) has shown that continuous experience is segmented into discrete events and sub-events, which aid real-time comprehension, memory, and decision-making. Computational neuroscience research observes and models ongoing brain activity as a series of stable population activity that occur across wide spatial and temporal scales, referred to as metastable neural activity (MNA). Through this review, we show that these isolated branches of literature, the cognitive theory of Event Segmentation (ES) and the mechanistic approach of metastability (MNA), actually study the same metastable neural states from different perspectives. While the behavioural branch offers a theory for the cognitive and behavioural utility of segmentation, the metastability literature provides the mechanistic account at the implementational level. We describe how metastable neural states act as the fundamental computational units of cognition and identify a number of core principles of how they operate. One is the spatio-temporally nested hierarchy of states, where longer-duration states in higher-order regions both constrain and are shaped by states in faster-operating regions. Another is that neural states are a reflection of underlying predictive models which shape perception, decision making, memory encoding and recall. And finally that neural states are periods of more modular processing, which are interspersed by boundaries where there is a reconfiguration of connectivity. Understanding how neural states emerge, interact, and shape cognition brings us closer to understanding the brain in its natural mode of operation.

AI.tldr: This review bridges two separate research fields—Event Segmentation (ES) from cognitive neuroscience and Metastable Neural Activity (MNA) from computational neuroscience—showing they study the same metastable neural states from different perspectives. It identifies core principles: spatio-temporally nested hierarchical states, predictive models underlying neural states, and modular processing with reconfiguration at boundaries.
AI.motivation: To unify two isolated branches of literature (cognitive ES theory and computational MNA) that study the same phenomenon of metastable neural states, and to articulate core principles of how these states operate as fundamental computational units of cognition.
AI.method: This is a review paper that synthesizes findings from cognitive, behavioural, and computational neuroscience, comparing the Event Segmentation framework with the metastable neural activity approach to reveal their common ground.
AI.result: Identifies three core principles: (1) spatio-temporally nested hierarchy where longer-duration states constrain and are shaped by faster-operating states; (2) neural states reflect underlying predictive models shaping perception, decision-making, and memory; (3) neural states are periods of modular processing interspersed by boundaries of connectivity reconfiguration.
AI.conclusion: Metastable neural states are fundamental computational units of cognition, and understanding their emergence, interaction, and shaping of cognition brings us closer to understanding the brain's natural mode of operation.

title: Reinterpreting Safety Thresholds as Neuron Spiking Thresholds
summary: Surrogate Safety Measures (SSMs) are extensively utilised in the evaluation of traffic risk in automated driving contexts. However, the majority of SSM-based evaluations employ fixed thresholds that fail to capture the human response to sustained borderline conditions or the reaction to brief, high-risk peaks. The present work proposes a biologically inspired reinterpretation of SSM thresholds. This is modelled as spiking thresholds of leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons, with multiple SSM inputs combined into a spiking neural network (SNN). The SNN is trained to emit spikes that are aligned with human braking onsets. The training data was recorded in a controlled car-following experiment using the 3D-CoAutoSim platform with CARLA/Unreal and a 6-DOF motion platform, where induced critical events were generated. The results demonstrate that the learned spiking activity qualitatively aligns with braking behaviour across scenarios and captures reactions that are not consistently explained by threshold crossings alone. Analysis across participants further indicates that learned input thresholds remain relatively consistent, while learned decay factors encode different temporal sensitivities for the SSMs. The findings of this study indicate that spiking dynamics may serve as a mechanism to facilitate the convergence of objective SSMs with subjective human safety perception.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a spiking neural network (SNN) approach to reinterpret Surrogate Safety Measure (SSM) thresholds using leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neuron dynamics, trained to align with human braking behavior in car-following scenarios. Results show that spiking activity captures human-like reactions better than fixed thresholds, with consistent learned thresholds but varying temporal sensitivities across participants.
AI.motivation: Traditional SSM-based evaluations use fixed thresholds that fail to capture human responses to sustained borderline conditions or brief high-risk peaks, necessitating a more biologically plausible approach to bridge objective safety measures and subjective human perception.
AI.method: The authors model SSM thresholds as spiking thresholds of LIF neurons within an SNN, trained on data from a controlled car-following experiment using the 3D-CoAutoSim platform (CARLA/Unreal Engine with a 6-DOF motion platform), where induced critical events generated human braking onset data.
AI.result: Learned spiking activity qualitatively aligns with braking behavior across scenarios and captures reactions not consistently explained by threshold crossings alone. Learned input thresholds remain relatively consistent, while decay factors encode different temporal sensitivities for different SSMs across participants.
AI.conclusion: Spiking dynamics may serve as a mechanism to facilitate the convergence of objective SSMs with subjective human safety perception, offering a more biologically grounded approach to traffic risk evaluation.

title: Evolutionary Algorithm for Reservoir Learning and Yielding
summary: Reservoir computing, a type of recurrent neural network, is a promising approach for temporal learning as it separates dynamic processing from the trained readout layer. However, classical Echo State Networks (ESNs) often require task-specific tuning of their architecture and hyperparameters to achieve good performance. This paper introduces EARLY (Evolutionary Algorithm for Reservoir Learning and Yielding), a framework designed to evolve both the topology and hyperparameters of multi-reservoir ESNs. Inspired by the modular organisation of the brain, EARLY encodes architectures as graph-based genomes and applies crossover, mutation, and selection to discover effective configurations. Our goal is to create both generic architectures and tasks inducing generalization. The method is evaluated on temporal learning tasks from the CogScale dataset. Results show that evolved architectures outperform those obtained with random search on several tasks and exhibit structural differences depending on task difficulty: simpler tasks yield lightweight architectures, while more complex tasks favour richer modular organisations. These findings suggest that evolutionary search can help identify reusable reservoir structures for a broader range of temporal problems. The evolved architectures are further evaluated on a cross-situational learning dataset to assess their ability to adapt to new environments.

AI.tldr: EARLY is an evolutionary framework that optimizes both topology and hyperparameters of multi-reservoir Echo State Networks, outperforming random search on temporal tasks and showing task-dependent structural complexity.
AI.motivation: Classical ESNs require task-specific tuning of architecture and hyperparameters; the authors aim to automate this process and discover reusable, generic reservoir structures through evolution.
AI.method: The EARLY framework uses graph-based genomes to encode multi-reservoir ESN architectures, applies evolutionary operators (crossover, mutation, selection) inspired by modular brain organization, and evaluates on CogScale temporal learning tasks.
AI.result: Evolved architectures outperform random search on several CogScale tasks; simpler tasks produce lightweight architectures, while complex tasks yield richer modular structures; evolved architectures show adaptability on a cross-situational learning dataset.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary search can identify reusable reservoir structures for diverse temporal problems, and the discovered architectures exhibit task-difficulty-dependent modular organization.

title: Private Noise and Public Error in Collective Information Acquisition
summary: Collective information acquisition requires groups to combine personal evidence with social information while remaining coupled to the external state. Communication noise can affect this process, but the role of noise remains unclear. In an online experiment, 600 participants worked in four-person human groups estimating a room temperature across 25 rounds while receiving either faithful social information, comprehension noise in which each receiver saw independently perturbed social information, or production noise in which perturbations were stored before display and could be seen by multiple receivers. The thermometer cue was objectively veridical, but its reliability was subjectively uncertain and the unitless 50--250 room-temperature range created a task-induced conflict between displayed evidence and everyday temperature expectations. Production-noise groups spent more rounds tightly clustered around a wrong value than comprehension-noise groups (\(p=0.016\), group-level permutation). Production noise more often created a wrong common signal (\(p=0.025\), Fisher's exact test) and made that signal persist across more rounds (\(p=0.004\), permutation). Dynamic update models showed that production noise was not more harmful because people followed peers more strongly, but because the same peer influence acted on more correlated production-noise perturbations. Exploratory human analyses linked the mechanism to psychological patterns while a GPT-agent experiment clarified a boundary condition: GPT agents registered uncertainty through reduced confidence without reproducing human-scale production-noise vulnerability. Overall, noise did not simply degrade collective information acquisition. Comprehension noise could sometimes improve correction relative to the faithful control, whereas production noise could turn perturbations into common evidence and stabilize consensus on error.

AI.tldr: An online experiment with 600 participants in four-person groups showed that production noise (shared perturbations) creates persistent wrong consensus more than comprehension noise (independent perturbations), because production noise turns random errors into common evidence that stabilizes around incorrect values.
AI.motivation: To understand how different types of communication noise (comprehension vs. production) affect collective information acquisition, given that noise's role in group decision-making remains unclear.
AI.method: Online experiment with 600 participants in four-person groups estimating room temperature across 25 rounds under three conditions: faithful social information, comprehension noise (independent perturbations), and production noise (shared perturbations). Also used GPT-agent experiments for comparison.
AI.result: Production-noise groups clustered around wrong values more rounds (p=0.016), created wrong common signals more often (p=0.025), and made those signals persist longer (p=0.004). Mechanism was not stronger peer following but correlated perturbations. GPT agents showed reduced confidence but not human-scale vulnerability.
AI.conclusion: Noise does not simply degrade collective information acquisition—comprehension noise can improve accuracy, while production noise creates persistent consensus on error by turning noise into shared evidence.

title: Supervised Training Rapidly Degrades Early Visual Cortex Alignment Across Biologically Plausible Learning Rules
summary: Random, untrained neural networks consistently match or exceed trained networks in representational similarity to early visual cortex. This puzzling finding challenges the assumption that learning improves brain alignment. We investigate it by tracking representational similarity analysis (RSA) alignment to human fMRI data across training for four learning rules: backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP). Using 720 object images from the THINGS database and fMRI data from three subjects across six visual ROIs, we measure Spearman correlations between model and brain representational dissimilarity matrices at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0-40). We find that (1) a single epoch of training reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%, depending on the learning rule; (2) backpropagation reduces V1 alignment most severely (delta r = -0.080), while predictive coding and STDP preserve substantially more (delta r ~ -0.04); and (3) a weaker, opposite tendency appears in object-selective cortex (LOC), where BP shows the largest increase in alignment during training, although the absolute change is small. These results suggest that untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases alone, and that global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

AI.tldr: Untrained neural networks match or exceed trained networks in similarity to early visual cortex. Training with backpropagation severely reduces V1 alignment (delta r = -0.080), while local learning rules (PC, STDP) better preserve brain-like representations (delta r ~ -0.04). The opposite trend appears in higher-level LOC.
AI.motivation: To understand why random, untrained neural networks show better representational alignment with early visual cortex than trained networks, challenging the assumption that training improves brain alignment.
AI.method: Track RSA alignment to human fMRI data (3 subjects, 6 visual ROIs, 720 THINGS images) across training (epochs 0-40) for four learning rules: backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP).
AI.result: (1) One epoch of training reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%. (2) BP reduces V1 alignment most severely (delta r = -0.080), while PC and STDP preserve more (delta r ~ -0.04). (3) Opposite weak trend in LOC where BP shows largest alignment increase.
AI.conclusion: Untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases; global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

title: Participation Costs Narrow Democratic Cooperation
summary: Collective action often requires institutions that make cooperation individually worthwhile. We ask whether democratic allocation of public-good return can transform a repeated public good into a self-sustaining cooperative institution, and how participation costs reshape that process. A simple evolutionary model shows that voted redistribution can support a prosocial allocation order, but can also sustain an antisocial allocation order or democratic free riding, in which individuals benefit from an institution maintained by others while avoiding the cost of participation. The model predicts competing effects of voting cost. Cost can suppress use of the institution to reward low contributors under strong selection, but can also thin the active electorate and erode contributor-rewarding support. We test these predictions in a preregistered online experiment with \NIncludedGroupsVone{} five-person groups. Endogenous democratic redistribution increased contributions relative to an equal-share public-goods control, with zero-cost voting producing the strongest temporal improvement. Voting costs did not mainly turn active voters toward low-contributor-rewarding allocation. Instead, they shifted behavior toward abstention and democratic free riding, made abstention locally rewarding, and widened the gap between post-task perceptions of democratic participation and the behavioral record. Democratic allocation can therefore stabilize cooperation, but participation costs can reduce the number of people actively sustaining the institution and can make that erosion less visible to participants themselves.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how democratic redistribution of public-good returns affects cooperation, finding that voting can sustain cooperation but participation costs lead to abstention, democratic free riding, and a gap between perceived and actual participation.
AI.motivation: Collective action requires institutions making cooperation individually worthwhile; the authors ask whether democratic allocation of public-good returns can create self-sustaining cooperative institutions and how participation costs affect this process.
AI.method: The authors develop a simple evolutionary model predicting prosocial/antisocial allocation orders and democratic free riding, then test predictions in a preregistered online experiment with five-person groups using endogenous democratic redistribution compared to equal-share public-goods control.
AI.result: Endogenous democratic redistribution increased contributions relative to control, with zero-cost voting showing the strongest improvement. Voting costs led to abstention, democratic free riding, locally rewarding abstention, and a gap between perceived participation and actual behavior.
AI.conclusion: Democratic allocation can stabilize cooperation, but participation costs reduce the number of active institutional sustainers and make this erosion less visible to participants themselves.

title: CellBRIDGE: Learning Cellular Trajectories via Interaction-Aware Alignment
summary: Inferring dynamics from population snapshots is a fundamental challenge in machine learning and biology. In scRNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq), destructive measurements preclude direct tracking of individual cells across time, making trajectory inference underdetermined. Optimal Transport (OT) provides a principled framework for snapshot alignment, but a long-standing modeling question is which cost functions yield biologically meaningful couplings. Standard OT approaches rely on gene-expression distances, implicitly treating cells as independent points and neglecting structured cell-cell communication mediated by ligand-receptor signaling. We introduce CellBRIDGE (Cell-Based Regularized Interaction-Driven Gene Expression), which augments feature-based OT with a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand-receptor activity. By explicitly modeling cell-cell communication, CellBRIDGE improves cross-snapshot couplings and downstream trajectory estimates across synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets relative to feature-only baselines. Notably, CellBRIDGE enables mechanistically interpretable in silico perturbations: on lung cancer data, silencing specific ligand-receptor pairs induces trajectory shifts that recapitulate expected effects of targeted pathway inhibition.

AI.tldr: CellBRIDGE improves optimal transport for single-cell trajectory inference by incorporating ligand-receptor interaction costs, yielding better cell couplings and enabling interpretable in silico perturbation analysis.
AI.motivation: Standard optimal transport methods for scRNA-seq trajectory inference rely on gene-expression distances, treating cells as independent points and ignoring structured cell-cell communication via ligand-receptor signaling.
AI.method: CellBRIDGE augments feature-based optimal transport with a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand-receptor activity to model cell-cell communication explicitly.
AI.result: CellBRIDGE improves cross-snapshot couplings and downstream trajectory estimates over feature-only baselines on synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets, and enables mechanistically interpretable in silico perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating ligand-receptor interaction costs into optimal transport improves trajectory inference and enables biologically meaningful perturbation analysis, as demonstrated on lung cancer data.

title: Spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based learning for infectious disease forecasting
summary: Spatio-temporal graph-based models have typically been used to forecast new cases of infectious diseases such as COVID-19 and chickenpox outbreaks. However, the use of stochastic modelling into their learning process has been surprisingly under-investigated and rarely considered entire data sets of large countries. As a result, it is unknown whether these models would provide accurate forecasts in real-world disease spread scenarios. In this work, we propose a spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based architecture that integrates a stochastic formulation and uncertainty approximation process to forecast new infectious disease cases. We find that our approach can adapt to encode large and small population geographical networks within a single model architecture. Using two real-world data sets, COVID-19 in the US and chickenpox in Hungary, we report an enhanced effect of the proposed architecture across predictions of the 2022 first wave for COVID-19 in the US and comparative results of chickenpox waves during 2012-2014 in Hungary. By benchmarking with four spatio-temporal graph-based models, quantitative results show competitive overall weekly performance of the proposed approach on forecasting new cases for all 3,218 US counties and all 20 Hungary counties. The proposed approach can represent overall epidemic progression relative to baselines, though with a one-step delay; while exhibiting a reduced sensitivity to high-frequency and low-amplitude variability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based architecture that integrates stochastic modeling and uncertainty approximation to forecast infectious disease cases (COVID-19 in US, chickenpox in Hungary). It shows competitive performance against four baselines across all US counties and Hungarian counties.
AI.motivation: Stochastic modeling has been under-investigated in spatio-temporal graph-based disease forecasting, and existing models rarely consider entire large-country datasets. It's unknown if such models provide accurate forecasts in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: A spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based architecture that incorporates a stochastic formulation and uncertainty approximation process within a single model, capable of encoding both large (US) and small (Hungary) population geographical networks.
AI.result: Competitive weekly performance across all 3,218 US counties (COVID-19) and 20 Hungarian counties (chickenpox), with enhanced prediction for the 2022 first wave in US and comparative results for 2012-2014 in Hungary. However, the approach shows a one-step delay and reduced sensitivity to high-frequency/low-amplitude variability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stochastic graph-based approach can effectively represent overall epidemic progression across different scales, outperforming baselines in overall trend representation, though with a trade-off of one-step delay and reduced sensitivity to fine-grained fluctuations.

title: What makes an action sequence enjoyable to watch?
summary: People often seek out ways to watch others perform complex action sequences (e.g., sports). What makes some sequences more enjoyable to watch than others? We generated 24 video clips of gameplay from a Flappy Bird-style video game. Clips varied in difficulty (how often players succeeded on average) and in moment-to-moment uncertainty (how likely the player was to crash at any given step). Participants (N=864) rated each video on one of three dimensions: how much they enjoyed it, how difficult the level appeared, or how dangerous the player's trajectory appeared. We found that participants preferred videos where the player seemed to be completing more difficult obstacle courses, but dangerousness did not predict enjoyment ratings. These findings show how procedurally generated stimuli can isolate the factors that affect how enjoyable an action sequence is to watch.

AI.tldr: A study using Flappy Bird-style gameplay videos found that people enjoy watching more difficult action sequences, but moment-to-moment uncertainty (dangerousness) does not predict enjoyment.
AI.motivation: To understand what makes watching complex action sequences (like sports) enjoyable to viewers.
AI.method: Generated 24 gameplay video clips varying in difficulty and moment-to-moment uncertainty. 864 participants rated clips on enjoyment, difficulty, or dangerousness.
AI.result: Participants preferred videos with higher difficulty, but dangerousness did not predict enjoyment ratings.
AI.conclusion: Procedurally generated stimuli can isolate factors affecting enjoyment of watching action sequences; difficulty matters more than perceived danger.

title: Tree Containment Parameterized by Scanwidth
summary: TREE CONTAINMENT is a central decision problem in mathematical phylogenetics, asking whether a given rooted phylogenetic tree is embeddable in ("displayed by") a given rooted phylogenetic network. While the problem is NP-complete for general networks, many algorithmic advances have relied on structural parameters that capture how "tree-like" a network is. In this paper we investigate TREE CONTAINMENT under the structural parameter scanwidth, a directed width measure generalizing popular parameters measuring tree-likeness of phylogenetic networks. We first present a parameterized algorithm that solves the problem in $O(4^{k + k\log{k}} n + nm^2)$ time, where $n$ and $m$ are the numbers of nodes and arcs in the network and $k$ is the width of a given tree-extension. Complementing this upper bound, we prove a matching lower bound under the Exponential-Time Hypothesis (ETH), showing that there is no algorithm for TREE CONTAINMENT that runs in $2^{o(c\log{c})} n^{O(1)}$ time, even on binary inputs, where $c$ is the directed cutwidth of the input network, which upper-bounds the scanwidth $k$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies TREE CONTAINMENT in phylogenetic networks under the scanwidth parameter, presenting a parameterized algorithm with O(4^{k + k log k} n + nm^2) runtime and a matching ETH-based lower bound showing no 2^{o(c log c)} n^{O(1)} algorithm exists, where c is the directed cutwidth.
AI.motivation: TREE CONTAINMENT is NP-complete for general phylogenetic networks, and the authors aim to understand its complexity under structural parameters that measure how "tree-like" a network is, specifically scanwidth.
AI.method: The paper designs a parameterized algorithm using the scanwidth parameter (derived from a given tree-extension) and proves a matching ETH-based lower bound via directed cutwidth, which upper-bounds scanwidth.
AI.result: Algorithm runs in O(4^{k + k log k} n + nm^2) time. Lower bound shows no 2^{o(c log c)} n^{O(1)} time algorithm exists under ETH, even for binary inputs.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm is essentially optimal under ETH, and the parameterized complexity of TREE CONTAINMENT with respect to scanwidth is settled.

title: Derivation, Analysis and Simulation of a Spatio-Temporal Epidemiology Model with Memory
summary: In this paper, we propose an integro-differential model for the spatio-temporal evolution of infectious diseases with asymptomatic transmission. The model consists of a reaction-diffusion system with an integral memory term accounting for the distribution of the incubation period. We first analyze the asymptotic behavior and the properties of the integro-differential model. Then, we prove the local existence of a weak solution of the system by means of the Faedo-Galerkin method and a compactness argument. The model is applied to simulate the geographical evolution of a disease in Lebanon.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an integro-differential model for infectious diseases with asymptomatic transmission, combining reaction-diffusion equations with memory terms for incubation periods, analyzes its mathematical properties, and applies it to simulate disease spread in Lebanon.
AI.motivation: To model the spatio-temporal evolution of infectious diseases that accounts for asymptomatic transmission and incubation period distributions, which are often overlooked in simpler models.
AI.method: An integro-differential model combining reaction-diffusion system with an integral memory term for incubation periods; analyzed using asymptotic behavior, Faedo-Galerkin method, and compactness arguments for local existence of weak solutions.
AI.result: Proof of local existence of weak solutions for the system; successful application to simulate geographical evolution of a disease in Lebanon.
AI.conclusion: The proposed integro-differential model effectively captures asymptomatic transmission and incubation period dynamics, with demonstrated applicability to real-world disease spread scenarios.

title: Scalable Inference-Time Annealing with Surrogate Likelihood Estimators
summary: A long standing challenge in computational chemistry and biophysics is efficiently sampling the Boltzmann distribution of molecules. Advances in generative modeling have been proposed to address the limitations of conventional sampling techniques by eliminating the computational cost of simulation. A promising direction is iteratively finetuning diffusion models along a temperature ladder whereby training data is generated via importance sampling during inference-time annealing. Unfortunately, these methods require computing a divergence over the score field to estimate importance weights, rendering them intractable for larger systems. Here we present scalable inference-time annealing (SITA), which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model to facilitate fast surrogate likelihoods. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms. Our code is available at: https://github.com/countrsignal/sita.git

AI.tldr: SITA introduces a scalable inference-time annealing method that retrains flow-based models to sample molecular Boltzmann distributions across temperatures without costly divergence calculations, achieving SOTA on Alanine Dipeptide and Tripeptide.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based annealing methods for molecular sampling require computing intractable score field divergences for importance weights, making them impractical for larger molecular systems.
AI.method: SITA retrains flow-based models along a temperature ladder using an energy-based model to provide fast surrogate likelihoods, avoiding explicit divergence computations during inference-time annealing.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide, demonstrating effectiveness while eliminating costly divergence terms.
AI.conclusion: SITA offers a scalable and efficient alternative for Boltzmann distribution sampling that avoids intractable computations, with potential applicability to larger molecular systems.

title: Chem-PerturBridge: a harmonized compendium of small molecule perturbation transcriptomic effects
summary: Large perturbation models require training data encompassing chemical, cellular, and assay diversity. Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling, however, are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines. We introduce Chem-PerturBridge, a harmonized multi-dataset resource comprising over 37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. We use the resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets. Matched same-compound conditions generally show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings and magnitudes across most dataset pairs, often falling below same-context different-compound baselines. In contrast, logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable and usually exceeds these baselines. We further evaluate Chem-PerturBridge as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning. Under a compound-held-out OP3 evaluation split, embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge improve over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and the descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics. An extensive molecule-holdout evaluation across 11 datasets further shows that models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match those that are not. Chem-PerturBridge therefore supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

AI.tldr: Chem-PerturBridge is a harmonized multi-dataset resource of 37k+ compounds, 136 cell contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across 8 assay types, enabling cross-dataset evaluation and improved compound representation learning.
AI.motivation: Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling are fragmented across different technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines, hindering the development of large perturbation models that require diverse training data.
AI.method: The authors constructed Chem-PerturBridge by harmonizing multiple datasets with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. They evaluated matched-condition agreement across datasets and used it as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning.
AI.result: Matched same-compound conditions show weak agreement in logFC rankings/magnitudes across datasets but stable direction agreement. Embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and baseline models across multiple evaluation metrics and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Chem-PerturBridge supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data for improved compound representation learning.

title: Automated Prediction of Postoperative Pancreatic Fistula Using Preoperative Computed Tomography
summary: Postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF) is a serious complication after pancreatic resection, increasing morbidity, hospital stay, and healthcare costs. We present an automatic, end-to-end deep learning pipeline-from pancreatic segmentation to classification-for preoperative POPF risk estimation and stratification using preoperative CT scans. A data set with auto-segmented pancreas volumes and surgical outcomes was used to evaluate multiple architectures, including a custom lightweight 3D CNN baseline (CNN3D), R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18 models. Evaluation across multiple 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance. This approach offers a clinically valuable tool and a methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification, supporting improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an end-to-end deep learning pipeline that segments the pancreas from preoperative CT scans and classifies the risk of postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF), evaluated using multiple 3D CNN architectures including CNN3D, R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18.
AI.motivation: POPF is a serious complication after pancreatic resection, increasing morbidity, hospital stay, and healthcare costs, motivating the need for preoperative risk estimation.
AI.method: An automatic end-to-end deep learning pipeline combining pancreatic segmentation and risk classification using multiple 3D architectures (custom CNN3D, R(2+1)D ResNet-18, ResNet-MC3-18) on preoperative CT scans.
AI.result: Evaluation across multiple 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance for POPF risk estimation.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a clinically valuable tool and a methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification, supporting improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.
